RE: Hendrickson vs. Aqran, et. a1.
orange county Superj-or court case No. 603194
Request for Informal FPPC opi.nion
Dear Ms. Donovan:
This letter is a fo1Iow-up to a telephone conversation with
Scott Halfabrin of your office on February 22, 1990, and to re-
quest an informal opinion from the Fair Political- Practices Com-
rn j,ssion concerning two issues.
The first 
j-ssue is r,/hether the attorneys fees paid to defend
Mr. cameron cosgrove j.n the Quo warranto action to challenge his
entitlernent to office constitute expenditures or contributions
for political 
purposes under the Political Reform Act.
The second issue is whether a determination by the Cj.ty of
Irvine to pay those fees under Government code Section 995, which
concerns the duty to defend an act or omission within the scope
of enployment, exenpts the expenditures from being for political
purposes under the Political Refon0 Act.
Factual Backqround
We have previously sent your office pleadings in this case.
Mr. Ray Hendrickson, a taxpayer in the City of lrvine, sought to
enjoin the payment of or to collect as civiL penalties the at-
torneys fees being paid by the City of Irvine to defend the Quo
warranto action against Mr. Cosgrove.
Mr. cosgrove received the third highest number
the Irvine city council's elections of June 1988 j.n of
an votes in
election
for two vacant council seats. 
However, Larry Agran, a sitting
councilmember with two years to go in his term, ran for and was
elected to the newly created post of Mayor, thus leaving his
councilmember I s seat vacant.
4199 Campus Drive, Suite 700 o lrvine, CA 92715 ' (714)854-2520

filorgl, Bo""nu.*oidE;.
Ms. Kathryn E. Donovan, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Cornmission
February 27, ]-99O
Page Two
Under the provisions of the law exj-sting prior to the June
l-988 election, the candidate with the next highest number of
votes was to fil-1 the unexpired term of a councilmember elected
Mayor. In June of 1988, the Irvine voters also passed a Ineasure
(Measure D) which by its own terms was to apply to the June 1988
election. 
Measure D allowed voters to petition for a special
election for a seat vacated. In compliance with an ordinance
drafted by the City Attorney in anticipation of Measure Dts pas-
sage, more than 7? of the voters petitioned for a special elec-
tion. 
The signatures on the petitions i{ere certified by the city
clerk on Jul-y 5, 1988. The term of the Mayor and new council-mem-
bers comnenced on JuIy 20, 1988. Measure D was certifj.ed and
chartered by the Secretary of state on JuLy 15, l-988.
on Juty 13, 1988, Mr. Agran resigned from his seat as a city
councilmember. On JuLy 2ot 19a8, Mr. Agran commenced his term as
Mayor of the City of Irvine. 
on July 20, 1988, l,lrs. Miller and
Mrs. werner were sk/orn in to the two elected seats and Mr.
cosgrove assumed the office vacated by I,1r. Agran.
The City council refused to honor the petition to hold a
speciat election. 
and the Quo warranto action followed after
being certified by the Attorney Genera]. The sole issue in the
Quo warranto action vJas whether 1"1r. cosgrove was entit]ed to hold
the office vacated by Mr. Agran.
The Judge in the Quo warranto action, in his Memorandum
of Tentative Decision, has ruLed that Mr. Cosgrovers candidaters
oath vras a sufficient 
oath of office and therefore he was en-
titled 
to the office and duly elected to it on Jufy L3, 1988,
before Measure D was chaptered and became effective.
Enclosed are copies of tlro letters written to me by the
Michael D. Rubin for the City of Irvine. 
The first 
is dated
January L2, f99O, and encloses a copy of the verbatim excerpts of
Minutes of Special Meeting hefd in the Irvj,ne City Council on
August 23, 1-988, in which the City decided to defend Mr. Cosgrove
in the Quo Warranto actj-on. The second is dated February 16,
1990 and encloses a }4enorandum of Tentative Decision in the Quo
warranto action. 
I have discussed both of these Ietters with my
cfient. 
As a representative of the public and the taxpayers he
wishes sorne guidance froin the public agency which enforces the
Political Reforn Act about the merits of his case.

As a result of ny conversations with Mr. Hallabrin I under-
stand that an infornal opinion can be rendered within 2L days,
but in no event later than 40 days. My client s/ifI be guided by
the Comrnission's opinion on these two issues in his prosecution
of the case.
If I can provj-de any further information or documents con-
cerning the issues, pl,ease let ne know. Al-so note that I an
sending a copy of this letter to Michael D. Rubin at Rutan &
Tucker so that he rnay provide you with vrhatever information he
deems rel-evant to your decision.
Thank you in advance for your courtesy and cooperation.
Very truly yours,
flugh Breckenridge

we try to ansr,/er al"1 advice requests promptfy. Therefore,

to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to
inf6rnation needed. rf your request is for informal assistance,
we wil} answer it as quickly as r^/e can. (See commission
Regulation L8329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)
You also should be aware that your l-etter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper requeqt for dj-sclosure.
Very truly yours,

RE: Hendrickson vs. Aqran i
Orange county Superior Court Case Number 603194
Dear Ms. Donovan:
You nay recal1 that the above-referenced case was brought
under the Politica1 Ref orrn Act to enjoin, and also seek civil
Penal-ties, under Government Code Section 9l-0o5. The suit chal-
lenges the paynent of attorneys fees by the city of Irvine to
defend councilmember cameron cosgrove in a Quo warranto action.
He was seated as a councilmember following the June 1988
tvlunicipal Election.
On September 21, 1989 my office provided Notice to you of
the comrnencement of the Action as required by Section 91007 of
the Government code. On December 4, f989, our Application for a
Prel irninary rnjunction was finally heard by the orange county su-
perior Court in Department 2l- before the Honorable Eileen Moore.
The application was denied on the basis that we made an insuffi-
cient showing of irreparabl-e j.njur1r., and on the basis l--hat- the
court rdas not satisfied that we lrould succeed on the merits of
the case. The defendants are the city, the three councj-Imenbers
s/ho voted to pay the fees, and Councilmember cosgrove, who has
received more than $40,000 in 1egal fees to defend the case.
The Court denied defendants' request for a bond under
covernment code, Section 9l-012 because the city is providing a
defense f or thern under G.qlICIIEcIt--geqe, Section 995. The city's
defense of thj.s action is al-so based entirely on its alleged
duty to defend Councilrnernber cosgrove under Government code, sec-
tion 995.
4199 Campus Drive, Suite 700 o lrvine, CA 92715 c (714) 854-2520

H[roll',, B*.*k.^oidlo.
c)E
Ms. Kathryn Donovan, General counsel-
California Fair PoliticaL Practices commission

Page Two
The Fair Political 
Practices Commission has assignment in-
terest in this case. The attorneys for the individual council-
Menber Defendants as well as the city of Irvine have offered to
dismiss the case and to waive their clain for fees. 
I, there-
fore, am requestj-ng that the cornmission provide to me, as attor-
ney for plaintiff, 
a statement of its interest in the case. I
would like to know whether it wishes the action to proceed and
\^/hether it wishes to irrtervene on behalf of the Plaintiff 
in the
ft appears that the issues may be resol-ved by summary Ad-
judication and as a consequence, there is little 
further work in
terms of trial preparation to be done on the case. However, the
lega1 issues are significant regarding your agency's authority
under the Political 
Reforn Act as revised by Proposition 73.
Since ny office 
does not stand to benefit fron the civi-1
Penalties in the event that we prevail in the action, I would ap-
preciate your callinq me to discuss the issues. I wilf be avail-
abl"e to discuss the issues until December 20, L989 and then will
not be back in the office until the January 3, 1990.
Very truly yours,
I,AW OFFICES OF HUGH BRECKENRIDGE

Fair Political Practices Commission
General Counselrs Office
Attention: Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
428 rrJrr Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 
95804-0807
Re: Hendrickson v. Agran, €t a1.
Orange County Superior Court Case No. 60-31 -54
Request by Huqh Breckenridge for Informal Opinion
Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
f am writing to follow up on the February 27, 1990
letter sent by Mr. Breckenridge as to whether the City of
Irvine's 
payment of Councilmember Cosgrove's quo warranto
defense costs constitutes reportable and/or iIlegal 
campaign
contributions to Mr. Cosgrove. Mr. Breckenridge represents
the individual plaintiff 
in the above-referenced litigation
and this office 
represents the City of Irvine and the
individual city council members named as defendants.
Ivlr. Breckenridge's February 27, 1990 letter states that
certain of the court papers were previously sent to your
office. 
Not knowing what specific documents Mr. Breckenridge
mailed to your I have enclosed a copy of our Memorandum of
Points and Authorities (dated October 31, 1989 ) in opposition
to lr{r . Breckenri-dqe ' s motion f or a preliminary inj unction.
The motion sought to enjoin the City of Irvine's payment of
U'
t-'>
{i-i=


RUTAN & TUcKER
A.r.rORNEYS AT LAW
Fair PoliticaI Practices Commission
Attention: Scott HaIIabrin, Esq.
March 15, 1990
Page 2
Councilmember Cosgrove's defense costs incurred in connection
with a quo warranto action.
As demonstrated in the opposition papers (at pages 7-
14), the City was not onfy authorized to but had a Iegal duty
pursuant to 
Government Code Sectlon 995 to 
provide
Councilmember Cosgrove with a defense. A councilmember is
entitled to the benefits of a defense under Government Code
Section 995 as an incident of his or her office or employment
just like he or she is entitled to a salary. 
This 1s true
even for a defense to office hoLders who later are found to
be improperly in office. 
(See, for example, Chubbuck v.
wilson (1907) I51 CaI. 162, 168.I
As evidenced by the ruling in the quo warranto action
(which was enclosed in Mr. Breckenridgers letter), 
the court
ultimately determined that Mr. Cosgrove was properly in
office. 
Because Mr. cosgrove provided consideration (serving
as a Councilmember) for the payment of his defense costs and
because the payment was a statutory 
obligation, 
the
voluntariness necessary for a campaign contributj-on is
absent. 
It 
1s no more a campaign contribution than an
insurance company's obllgatory payment of defense costs under
an j-nsurance policy would be.
In ruling on Mr. Breckenridge's motion for a pre.Iiminary
lnjunction, the court addressed the merits of his case and
found that 
(among other things ) he had not shown a
robability of prevailinq on the merits of his Political
Reform Act allegations. 
( See enclosed December 4, 
1 989
Reporterrs Transcript at page 31 , l-ines 6-7.) 
It may be
notable to point out that before it authorized payment of
Councilmember Cosgrove's defense costs, the City Council was
advised by its City Attorney that covernment Code Section 995
required the City to pay such costs.
We can provide you with a detailed legal analysis if you
deem it helpful and appropriate. 
We also disagree with the
factual representatj,ons set forth in Mr. Breckenridge I s
fetter. 
Since we are not intimately familiar with the FppCrs


RUTAN & TUCKER
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
procedures in
inform us if
desired.

sections.question: The first issue is whether the attorneys fees paid to defend Mr. Cameron Cosgrove in the Quo warranto action to challenge his entitlement to office constitute expenditures or contributions for political purposes under the Political Reform Act. The second issue is whether a determination by the City of Irvine to pay those fees under Government Code Section 995, which concerns the duty to defend an act or omission within the scope of employment, exempts the expenditures from being for political purposes under the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether attorney's fees paid by the City of Irvine to defend councilmember Cameron Cosgrove in a quo warranto action constitute political expenditures under the Political Reform Act, and whether the city's duty to defend under Government Code Section 995 exempts such fees from being characterized as political expenditures.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple letters: (1) Feb 27, 1990 incoming request from Hugh Breckenridge requesting FPPC informal advice with detailed factual background; (2) Mar 6, 1990 FPPC acknowledgment letter confirming receipt; (3) Dec 12, 1989 letter (misdated or earlier correspondence) requesting FPPC statement of interest in the case; (4) Partial law firm letterhead from Rutan & Tucker at end. The main substantive request is the Feb 27, 1990 letter. Marked as 'incoming_request' because this is the lawyer's request to FPPC, not FPPC's advice response.

The second issue is whether a determination by the City of Irvine to pay those fees under Government Code Section 995, which concerns the duty to defend an act or omission within the scope of employment, exempts the expenditures from being for political purposes under the Political Reform Act.

embedding.summary: This document is a request for informal FPPC advice regarding whether attorney's fees paid by the City of Irvine to defend councilmember Cameron Cosgrove in a quo warranto action constitute political expenditures under the Political Reform Act. The document includes factual background about the 1988 Irvine city council election, Cosgrove's appointment to office, and the resulting legal challenge to his entitlement to the seat.
